title: Roast rack of lamb
subtitle: Herb-crusted French-trimmed rack, roasted pink and rested
excerpt: A French-trimmed rack of lamb coated in Dijon mustard and a herb-and-breadcrumb crust, roasted at high heat for around 20 minutes until pink at the centre. This is the most impressive thing a home cook can put on a dinner-party table for the amount of effort it requires.
sourceNotes: The French-trimmed rack of lamb with herb crust is a classic of the post-war British dinner-party table. Method follows the Escoffier approach to roasting small cuts: high heat, short time, proper resting. Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903, trans. Cracknell and Kaufmann) — carré d'agneau method.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Apply the mustard and crust up to 4 hours ahead, cover loosely, and refrigerate. Bring to room temperature 30 minutes before roasting.

content.text: Two racks of lamb, French-trimmed, serve four people: three cutlets each, served pink. The internal temperature to aim for is 57°C at the centre for medium-rare. If you do not have a thermometer, 18 to 20 minutes at 220°C in a fan oven achieves this for a standard 350 g rack. The resting time is as important as the roasting: 10 minutes on a warm plate, covered loosely with foil.

The Dijon mustard goes on first, before the crust. It acts as a glue for the breadcrumbs and adds a faint heat that suits the lamb. The herb mix is flexible: parsley, rosemary, and thyme is the standard British combination. Keep the crust thin — a thick coating steams rather than crisps.

Combine the {{breadcrumbs-fresh}} of breadcrumbs, {{parsley-flat}} of parsley, {{rosemary}} of rosemary leaves, {{garlic}} of garlic, and {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil in a bowl. Season with salt and pepper and mix until the crumbs are just coated. The mixture should hold together when pressed but not feel wet.

Heat the oven to 220°C (fan 200°C). Season the racks all over with salt and pepper. Heat a dry frying pan over high heat until very hot. Sear the racks fat-side down for 2 minutes, then turn and sear the bone side for 1 minute. Transfer to a roasting pan, bones arching upward. Brush the fat side with {{dijon-mustard}} of Dijon mustard and press the herb crust firmly over the mustard in an even layer.

Roast for 18 to 20 minutes for medium-rare. A thermometer inserted into the centre of the meat, away from the bone, should read 57°C. Transfer to a warm plate, cover loosely with foil, and rest for 10 minutes. Carve into individual cutlets between the bones and serve immediately.

items.symptom: Crust has slid off during roasting
items.cause: Mustard layer too thin, or crust too dry
items.fix: Press the crust firmly onto the mustard; a generous smear of mustard helps the crumbs adhere; do not shake or jostle the roasting pan

items.symptom: Meat is pink at the bone but grey near the surface
items.cause: Oven temperature too high or rack positioned too close to the element
items.fix: Roast at 220°C on the middle shelf; the bone insulates the meat and can cause uneven cooking if positioned poorly

items.cause: Roasted too long, or racks were cold from the fridge
items.fix: Bring the racks to room temperature for 30 minutes before roasting; use a thermometer rather than time alone

items.cause: Crumbs too fine or too much oil, or oven too low
items.fix: Use slightly coarser crumbs; reduce the oil slightly; switch the oven to grill for the final 2 minutes if the crust remains pale

content.text: The herb-crusted rack of lamb became a British dinner-party fixture in the 1970s and 1980s, as the French style of cooking individual portions at high heat for speed and drama spread into the domestic kitchen. It sits at the crossroads of French technique and British roasting tradition. Spring is its natural season — Welsh and English lamb arrives in spring, and the dish has a lightness that suits the first warm evening of the year. The crossed rib bones, stripped clean, serve no culinary purpose; they are purely ceremonial, and the ceremony is appropriate.
